items: Secure Your Bedroom Door: Install heavy-duty door sweeps on your bedroom door to close gaps your cat might squeeze through or scratch open during vulnerable nighttime hours.
Add Secondary Barriers: Set up baby gates with opaque covers at bedroom or hallway entrances if your cat has demonstrated door aggression behavior.
Designate a Panic Room: Choose one cat-free room in your home as your retreat space where you can escape safely when an attack begins, and test that the lock works properly.
Equip Your Panic Room: Place a flashlight, phone charger, and slippers inside your designated panic room so you can reach safety and regroup during nighttime incidents.
Prepare Distance Tools: Keep a thick blanket, towel, spray bottle, or compressed air canister within arm's reach of your bed to create space between you and an attacking cat.
Address Bedroom Vulnerabilities: Before sleeping each night, check whether your cat can open your door, squeeze underneath it, or reach through any gaps to access you.
Understand Peak Risk Hours: Recognize that attacks most frequently occur between 2 a.m. and 5 a.m. when your cat's crepuscular biology primes them for activity and you are most vulnerable.
Assess Night Access Routes: Identify every pathway your cat might use to reach you during the night and install barriers or locks to block each route before you sleep.
Document Attack Patterns: Keep a detailed log of when attacks occur, what preceded them, and their severity so you can identify triggers and share patterns with professionals.
Rule Out Medical Causes: Schedule a veterinary appointment to check for underlying pain or illness that might be intensifying your cat's aggression and reactivity.
Consult a Behaviorist: Contact a board-certified veterinary behaviorist or certified animal behaviorist when environmental modifications alone are insufficient to manage your cat's condition safely.

Set Decision Milestones: Establish clear criteria for evaluating whether management is improving your cat's quality of life or whether professional intervention or difficult decisions may be necessary.
